one_line_takeaway: Adversarial training measurably hardens deepfake detectors under white-box/cross-model conditions but its benefit shrinks and can turn actively harmful (negative AMR) once attacker and defender diverge in both dataset and model architecture, so AT alone cannot be trusted as a deployed defense against realistic transfer-based attacks.
key_finding: (1) Attack effectiveness (Table 1, ASR%): PGD is strongest across all mismatch levels -- C1 (white-box) 99.6%, C3 (cross-model) 89.8%, C5 (cross-dataset) 65.1%, C7 (cross-dataset+cross-model) 66.7%. FGSM: C1 71.7%, C3 56.5%, C5 48.4%, C7 49.2%. FPBA: C1 85.8%, C3 59.9%, C5 45.5%, C7 51.5%. Transferability persists (~50% ASR) even under full mismatch. (2) AT preserves nominal performance (Table 2, AUC): all 11 AT strategies keep FF++ AUC >=94.3% (Base 95.4%) and Celeb-DF-V2 AUC in 81.7-82.9% (Base 79.8%) -- no catastrophic overfitting observed. (3) AT robustness under mismatch (Table 3): in-distribution AT gains are large -- e.g. PGD AT drops C1 ASR from 99.6% (Base 85.7% average-baseline) to 20.2% (AMR 79.7%), and Ensemble AT reaches 1.1% ASR (AMR 98.7%) in C1. But under cross-dataset conditions (C5/C7), gains collapse and several strategies show NEGATIVE AMR (AT increases vulnerability), e.g. FGSM C5 AMR -36.6%, FGSM C7 -34.1%, FGSM_surr C5 -21.5%, Ens C5 -11.5%. FPBA_surr achieves the lowest ASR in C7 (48.9%, AMR 5.1%), suggesting task-specific (frequency-domain) attack diversity generalizes best cross-dataset. (4) Leave-one-out ablation (Table 4): omitting any single attack from ensemble AT raises ASR in every DUMB case; omitting FPBA (training on PGD+FGSM only) is worst under cross-dataset settings (C5 64.1%, C7 66.2%), confirming frequency-domain attack coverage is uniquely important for generalization.
core_contribution: The paper (Serrano, Umlil, Thomas — Thales) extends the DUMB/DUMBer transferability-mismatch methodology (originally from prior work, refs [2],[3]) to the deepfake-detection domain for the first time. It builds a benchmark spanning five deepfake detectors (Xception, UCF, RECCE, SPSL, SRM), three adversarial attacks (PGD, FGSM, FPBA), two datasets (FaceForensics++ and Celeb-DF-V2), and eleven adversarial-training (AT) strategies (single-attack, dual, triple/Ensemble, and surrogate-diversified variants), evaluated across four DUMB mismatch cases: C1 white-box, C3 cross-model, C5 cross-dataset, C7 cross-dataset+cross-model. The study runs 32 test sets x 12 training strategies x 5 architectures = 1920 evaluations to quantify how adversarial training's robustness benefit holds up (or collapses) under realistic attacker/defender mismatch rather than idealized white-box assumptions.
threat_model: Deepfake-detection models deployed as classifiers (real vs. fake face image) are attacked by an adversary crafting imperceptible Lp-bounded perturbations (l-infinity budget 9/255) using transfer-based (black-box) attacks: the attacker trains a surrogate model (possibly a different architecture and/or trained on a different dataset than the deployed defender model) and transfers adversarial examples to the target. Three attack families are used: FGSM (single-step gradient sign), PGD (iterative, alpha=4/255, 4 steps, random start, via TorchAttacks), and FPBA (Frequency-based Post-train Bayesian Attack, combining spatial and frequency-domain artifacts, K=3 appended models, lr=0.001, N=5 spectrum transforms, 4 iterations). The defender's mitigation is adversarial training (retraining from scratch with 80% clean / 20% adversarial samples, adversarial subset split across multiple attacks/surrogates when combined). The paper explicitly frames white-box AT evaluation as unrealistic and studies attacker-defender knowledge/data mismatch as the core threat variable.
limitations: The paper's own conclusion and appendix note: (a) only two datasets (FF++, Celeb-DF-V2) and one perturbation budget/attack parameterization were tested; future work should include more attacks/datasets; (b) AT is shown to be 'necessary but insufficient' under cross-dataset mismatch -- robustness benefits are highly sensitive to train/test distribution alignment and can turn negative; (c) results are reported as averages across the five detectors in the main tables, obscuring per-model variance (though per-model detail is given in Appendix Tables 5-9, which show e.g. SPSL and SRM retain markedly higher residual ASR to certain attacks after AT than other architectures); (d) no additional defenses beyond adversarial training (e.g., input transformation, detection-based defenses, media compression robustness) are evaluated; (e) the study does not test adaptive attacks that are aware of the AT defense being used.

quantitative_results: PGD ASR by DUMB case: C1 99.6%, C3 89.8%, C5 65.1%, C7 66.7% (Table 1)
FGSM ASR by DUMB case: C1 71.7%, C3 56.5%, C5 48.4%, C7 49.2% (Table 1)
FPBA ASR by DUMB case: C1 85.8%, C3 59.9%, C5 45.5%, C7 51.5% (Table 1)
Nominal AUC after AT (Table 2): FF++ Base 95.4% to Ens_Surr 94.3% (range 94.3-95.5%); Celeb-DF-V2 Base 79.8% to Ens_Surr 82.9% (range 81.7-82.9%)
FGSM AT shows negative AMR under cross-dataset: C5 -36.6%, C7 -34.1% (Table 3)
Leave-one-out (Table 4): omitting FPBA from AT (training PGD+FGSM only) raises test ASR to C5 64.1%, C7 66.2% -- worse than full ensemble
Total evaluation scale: 32 test sets x 12 training strategies x 5 architectures = 1920 evaluations
Diversity-driven AMR improvements of 26% up to 83% in gray-box (cross-model) settings, per Conclusion
baselines_compared: Dual-attack AT (PGD+FGSM, PGD+FPBA, FGSM+FPBA, used in leave-one-out)
Ens_Surr (all three attacks x all surrogates)
